column9: HighNoon rides into town with three performers, no script, and some balloons. Together with the audience, they build a western in real time, where every balloon pop is a gunshot, and no one knows when the next one is coming.
column10:
HighNoon is a long-form improvised western, but the guns are balloons, and both the audience controls when they go off. At the top of the show, we ask for two simple suggestions: a western location, and a problem that might happen there. From that, we build a character-driven story inspired by classic frontier films — lawmen, outlaws, townsfolk, and the fragile communities they create.
Three balloons are given to the audience in full view at the start of the show.  When any balloon pops, a gun has fired in the world of the play — and the performers must instantly justify what that gunshot means for the story: a missed shot, a warning, a death, a twist, or something no one could have planned. The result is a show that has tension, levity, drama, and loss at every minute!

column12: Something we don’t tell the audience about our act is that all the performers have balloons on them too. After the last balloon pops, we want to keep surprising them with more “action”. We want to keep that moment pure for our audiences, so we ask that no photos, videos, or other media of the performers with balloons in their hands be used for marketing before or after the festival.

column9: Nicole Bindler’s performances have been supported by numerous grants and fellowships, and presented on four continents. Recent projects include curating an evening of Palestinian dance films; somatic research on the embryology of the genitalia from a non-binary perspective; and a solo clown-dance, “The Case for Invagination”, in which her scars speak candidly about trauma and desire. Notable performance projects have included touring as a choreographer with the Bethlehem-Based Palestinian company, Diyar Theatre; a Pew-funded performance residency in Japan including Butoh training with Yoshito Ohno; and performing Deborah Hay's “I Think Not” at Unidad De Vinculación Artística in Mexico City. At age 48 she decided to start doing stand-up.
column10: After many years in dance-theater Nicole Bindler is making a major departure into stand-up comedy! She’s been studying the form and has written a 20 minute set called, “Dancers Are Like Butterflies: magnificent, rarely seen, only last 20 days”. The set explores the challenges of being a dance-maker and educator in the U.S. today. Bindler reflects on the problems dancers face through comedy to better understand the conditions and laugh at the absurdity of toiling for little to no money to make beautiful things for the stage that very few people will ever see. Her comedy also addresses dance and somatic education, aging, and political differences among dancers. Bindler creates a container for dancers’ rage, disappointments, and dreams, and gives non-dancer audiences a window into what it is like for many dance-makers.
column11: I’ve been involved in dance-comedy and clowning for years, but I’m newer to stand-up. I feel the interconnections between these forms, but they’re often sequestered into separate events. I’m excited about participating in and attending an interdisciplinary comedy festival because I will get to meet comedians of all sorts and learn new ways of engaging in comedic performance.

column9: Local Spot has been a cornerstone of Baltimore's improv community since 2015, making them one of the city's most established and experienced ensembles (including artistic directors, teachers, and house performers from three different improv theaters in the region). Their unique approach to the monoscene format is both fast-paced and character based. Local Spot has performed consistently throughout Baltimore since its inception, as performing regularly at festivals including the Detroit Improv Festival, San Diego Improv Festival, SteelStacks Improv Festival, XL Fest, and Del Close Marathon.
column10: Local Spot has been dedicated to the monoscene format for its entire decade of existence. Unlike most other improv formats, their show takes place entirely in one location, at one time, with no edits, jumps, cuts, or other improv trickery. Performers are forced to live with the decisions they’ve made for the entire show without the ability to escape in an edit. Expect a character- and emotion-driven show in which anything can happen, and the improvisers have to deal with it. Our opening has an audience volunteer picking a color from a paint swatch book and painting the scene inspired by the name of the paint color (always fun and evocative). Like many monoscenes, our shows are heavily character driven, but Local Spot is unique in how we use frequent entrances and exits to create monoscenes that at the same time are fun, fast-paced, and have compelling narratives. Secrets come out, alliances are made and broken, and someone usually falls in love. It’s always a compelling rollercoaster!

column9:
Birds & Broads is a powerhouse female duo featuring Liz Hutchman and Jacque Arend, whose creative partnership has spanned over 15 years. Currently a featured house team at Second Beat Improv Theater in Phoenix, the duo are the veteran co-creators behind Arizona's SAVI FEST and Sonoran All-Valley Improv along with Krissy Lenz (Neighborhood Comedy Theater, Mesa, AZ).

After winning the Phoenix Improv March Madness Playoff in 2014, Birds & Broads took their signature style of performance and teaching on the road, touring throughout California and appearing most recently at the Tucson Comedy Arts Festival (2022, 2023). Known for their deep chemistry and communal impact, Liz and Jacque continue to be a driving force in the Southwest improv scene.
column10:
Driven by a single audience suggestion, Birds & Broads weaves an organic tapestry of shifting themes, vivid locations, and complex relationship dynamics. Toggling effortlessly between the grounded and the absurd, committing to each reality, they hold a mirror to the human condition through a fresh, high-energy performance that is revealed moment to moment.

Birds & Broads utilizes a "rolling inspiration" technique to launch an organic, long-form set that prioritizes deep relational connection over rigid narrative control. By focusing on foundational scene work and "discovery-led" play, the duo builds expansive worlds where nuanced character dynamics spark high-level, spontaneous world-building.
column11: I love to travel, and I love improv - so it's a win win, I'm specifically submitting for Sear Jar Jamboree Comedy Improv Festival because it's an exciting new adventure for the producers and I'd love to be a part of it. I love Philly, and my partner Liz's parents still live in her childhood home in Ambler, PA. So I also have a home in Ambler, PA. Going to this fest, will be like going home for both of us. I look forward to connecting with camp friends and seeing how your community is growing.

column9: What happens when you mix decades of life experience with improv? column10: Blending personal storytelling with improvisation in a high-energy Armando format, Sticky Notes invites the audience into the conversation. Our format emphasizes authenticity and connection: stories are drawn from real lived experience across generations.

column10:
Description for the public:
Fresh off an incredible run at the world-famous Edinburgh Fringe Festival, Gillian Gurganus is back with her dazzling solo show, And All That Jazz, all about one triple threat's road to fame and stardom, one horrible, mind-numbing job at a time. Come on this journey and witness Gillian searching for her big break - in all the wrong places! 

More info for people reviewing these applications!
This is my storytelling comedy solo show that I wrote all about my journey to find my "big break" as a self proclaimed "triple threat." My style is Mike Birbiglia meets Catherine Cohen - my show is packed with written jokes, glitz, glam, and LOTS of vulnerability! If you've ever had a horrible job that you thought would fix all of your problems, then this show is for you! Also....I *might* even sing and dance at the end...like a true triple threat would!

column9: James Christy Jr. is an award-winning playwright, filmmaker, novelist and comedian. His one-hour stand-up show I THINK NOT premiered at the Coop Philadelphia in July 2025; his previous solo show NOT RIGHT NOW, had a sold-out run at the Philly Fringe in 2022. His play LOVE AND COMMUNICATION won the Brown Martin Barrymore Award in 2012 has been made into a feature film now available on Amazon and AppleTV. He is currently in a residency at the Broadway Comedy Club in NYC. In a previous life he was an actor with a featured role in DEAD POETS SOCIETY (yes, he stood on his desk).
column10:
I Think Not is a solo comedy show built around one man’s dysfunctional relationship with his inner monologue. 
Many male-centered solo shows have a certain trajectory: man makes mistakes, man learns from mistakes, man presents his personal growth before an audience to thunderous applause. Early in I Think Not, Jim promises the audience they will witness no personal growth during the evening. And he delivers on that promise.
The show is about the stories we tell ourselves to get through life. And how, from the Old Testament to Instagram Reels, male storytellers in particular create self-serving narratives that blame others and try to cover over our inherent weakness. This plays out in Jim’s complicated and fundamentally dishonest relationships with his wife, his dog, his phone and alcohol.

I hosted an open mic for 8 months and I am a part of my university’s only non audition gender minority collegiate sketch comedy club as a writer and actor. I love doing standup because I think comedy can be a great way to bring up the mood and connect with people.
column10:
My standup comedy style is a great balance between witty and dry humor. Often, my sets are based on situations or observations of the common world.  I enjoy setting up each joke with a story, sprinkling in punchlines throughout. I try to end every set with a big bow, tying multiple punchlines together for closure. My sets are typically clean, in the sense that I send my parents every set I have done, and then they send it to all their friends. 
I really enjoy talking about things that have happened to me or my friends or funny things I have observed in the world. Most recently, I have been studying abroad and collected many stories about the culture shock of it all.

column9: I’m a Houston-based comedian who’s been performing for the past four years, bringing a high-energy, animated style to the stage. My comedy is rooted in honesty and my life experiences. I love that stand-up gives me a space where I don’t have to sugarcoat how I feel. What you see is what you get: expressive storytelling, big reactions, and real perspectives shaped by my experiences.
column10:
My show is a high-energy, personality-driven stand-up set that blends animated storytelling with sharp, honest observations. I lean into expressive delivery and physicality, bringing each story to life in a way that feels vivid and engaging. The material is rooted in real experiences and unfiltered thoughts, creating a conversational feel where the audience gets pulled directly into my perspective.
The format is primarily stand-up, but it thrives on connection and momentum—I play off the room, react in the moment, and let my energy build throughout the set. The goal is to create a space where people can laugh freely at the truth, even when it’s a little uncomfortable, and leave feeling like they’ve experienced something genuine, not rehearsed or watered down.

column9:
Tom Achilles is a New York-based comedian, writer, filmmaker, and actor. His eclectic style blends elements of surrealism, stream of consciousness, and wordplay to satirize the absurdities of modern life. This approach is showcased in his 2025 comedy special Mindbender, produced by Pure Chaos Comedy, as well as his 2022 comedy album Guillotine. Tom’s sense of humor is also highlighted in Zeitgeist Meetup, a book of gag cartoons he co-authored with New Yorker cartoonist Pat Achilles. You can hear more from him on Tom & Will Start A Podcast, where he and fellow comedian Will Purpura discuss outlandish startup ideas.

Achilles has also been involved in many projects across film and video. His short film Gone recently won “Best Experimental Short” at the AltFF Alternative Film Festival. In 2023, his digital comedy sketch “America’s Got Tom” was profiled by Vulture in their “Best Comedy Shorts” segment. Additionally, his comedy show Ye Ol’ Triple Crown was highlighted in a video from VICE about surviving on a $0 budget for a full week in NYC.

Tom’s writing and voice acting can also be seen in a variety of publications. Most recently, he wrote and voiced a character for the new Troma Entertainment video game Poultrygeist. Additionally, his humor pieces have been featured in McSweeney’s, Points in Case, Buzzfeed, and The Weekly Humorist.

Achilles has performed original work at popular NYC establishments such as Joe’s Pub, The Bell House, Union Hall, Littlefield, and The Slipper Room. He’s also been an official selection of Solocom, The Very Normal Festival, 5th Borough Comedy Festival, and Philly Fringe’s Cannonball Festival. 

Originally from Bucks County, Pennsylvania, Tom attended American University in Washington, DC, where he earned a degree in Communications. While in college, he taught at Washington Improv Theater (WIT) and performed with house ensembles King Bee and Season Six. He is a Scorpio.
column10: Tom Achilles is a New York-based comedian known for his eclectic and unconventional style, blending surrealism, stream of consciousness, and mind-bending linguistics. His unorthodox style has been described as "dreamlike,"  "avant-bizarre," and "acid comedy." After you watch Achilles, you'll be unsure if you just witnessed stand-up, theater, or a controlled hallucination. Buckle up!

column10: Erik Moyer is a one-liner comic with sharp wit, dark twists, and a butt that just won't quit. Expect a performance that's tight, fast, and dizzying like a loop-de-loop rollercoaster: Expect jokes that are wry, dry, and dark like a black Manhattan. We welcome people to the Temple of Boom, tell them how to participate, and then we start taking suggestions to inspire one song at a time. Cat uses a piano and guitar. A nearby electrical outlet and three chairs is all we need. If the house has a piano, great, if not, we will bring one. 
These songs are anywhere from 2 to 5 minutes long. If we're given a set-length that exceeds  25 minutes,  we often add two of our original music-based long-form games. But they're kept a surprise, because everything is weird in this show.

Get ready for a night of unapologetic, no-filter comedy with Naked Eddie—a performer who turns bad decisions, bold opinions, and brutally honest confessions into laugh-out-loud storytelling.

Blending sharp wit with just the right amount of chaos, Naked Eddie dives headfirst into the kind of topics most people are too afraid to say out loud. His comedy walks the line between outrageous and relatable, mixing self-deprecating humor, wild storytelling, and a fearless stage presence that keeps audiences hooked from start to finish.

Expect big laughs, unexpected turns, and a show that feels equal parts stand-up and unhinged group chat. It’s raw, it’s ridiculous, and it’s completely unforgettable.

Come see why Naked Eddie is the comic people can’t stop talking about—for better or worse.

column9: FlyyFatBoi is a rising comedian bringing raw energy, real-life storytelling, and unfiltered humor to every stage. In just six months of doing comedy, he’s already accomplished three sold-out shows, secured private bookings, and earned a main stage performance—proving his ability to connect with audiences quickly. His style blends authenticity, relatability, and bold punchlines, making him a standout voice you won’t forget.
column10:
A FlyyFatBoi show is part stand-up, part storytelling, part “did he really just say that?” You come in expecting jokes and leave realizing you just laughed through real-life chaos, relationships, pain, and growth—without even noticing he was lowkey preaching the whole time.

It’s loud, it’s raw, it’s unpredictable. One minute you crying laughing, next minute you looking at your friend like “damn…that hit a little too close.”

It feels like kicking it with that one friend who says everything everybody else is scared to say—but somehow makes it funny instead of uncomfortable.

Basically… it’s comedy, but with a heartbeat.

column9: Problem Child is a Philadelphia-based improv troupe inspired by the question we all grew up hearing: “what the hell is wrong with you?” Starting from audience stories, they build worlds that feel familiar, messy, and just a little off, where small problems have a way of growing into something messier.
column10: Problem Child starts with a question most of us heard growing up: “what the hell is wrong with you?” An audience member shares a real problem, and from there the group builds a world that feels both familiar and just a little off. The piece is typically performed as a tight 20-minute set. It opens with a grounded, shared environment before expanding into a montage of scenes that revisit themes, characters, and patterns inspired by the initial suggestion. The group prioritizes emotional commitment, clear relationships, and strong point of view over purely game-driven play.
column11: As a relatively new team, the chance to perform at festivals is genuinely exciting for us—we’re still building our voice together, and every opportunity to get on stage matters. Just as important, though, is the chance to watch and learn. We’re drawn to seeing the wide range of styles, formats, and risks other groups are taking, especially the kinds of choices we wouldn’t think to make ourselves. Festivals feel like a space to grow quickly, be inspired, and come back sharper and more connected as a team.

column10:
A personal, story-driven stand-up set that blends sharp humor with self-aware, slightly dark storytelling. Drawing from relationships, emotional patterns, and growing up in the Hudson Valley, the material explores the uncomfortable, messy, and quietly absurd parts of being human.

The performance leans into vulnerability while poking at it—finding humor in bad habits, misconnection, and the kinds of thoughts we’re usually smart enough not to say out loud. It’s honest, a little chaotic, and lives somewhere between healing and making things worse, in a way that feels both intimate and fun.

column9: Gimpl the Fool is the resident Yiddish balloon puppeteer of Clown Heights, and a proud graduate of the Coney Island Clown Skool. Since their performance debut in August 2025, Gimpl has produced and performed a variety of experimental short-form one-clown acts using puppets and props made entirely out of balloons. They literally breathe life into every single puppet, constructing complex original balloon designs on-site for several hours before every single performance. They recently had the immense honor of becoming the first person in the 46-year history of Baltimore's Black Cherry Puppet Theater to perform balloon puppetry on stage.
column10:
Gimpl the Fool's Balluppet Theater is an ever-evolving series of short-form one-clown shows featuring puppets and props made entirely out of balloons. These comedic acts have taken the form of  simple puppet showcases, film parodies, live yiddish singing performances, and even magic shows. Gimpl employs a wide variety of puppets in these acts, ranging from single-balloon hand puppets to 4-balloon humanettes to massive glowing 120-balloon monstrosities, all of which are painstakingly crafted just hours before each performance.

The first installment in this series was a short musical showcase of several balloon puppet designs. It was soon followed by a balloon puppet parody of "It's the Great Pumpkin, Charlie Brown". After that, they developed their longest and most elaborate piece so far, "Fiddler on the Balloof", followed by their first attempt at the horror genre, an Alien spoof titled "In Space No One Can Hear You Squeak". The one constant in the Balluppet Theater is that nothing ever seems to go as planned.

For the folks reviewing applications: This would be the longest set length so far for Gimpl the Fool's Balluppet Theater, so I would most likely be creating an entirely new act just for this festival, but I may also want to try extending or stringing together one or more of my previous acts. As a result, I can't give you a precise description of *this* act, but it will definitely be produced with comedy in mind above all else.
column11: I've never performed at a festival before and I'm dying to finally perform in the glorious city of Philadelphia!
column12: If selected, I need as much time as possible at the venue on the day of the performance to construct my puppets and props on-site, as they only last a day before popping or deflating, and constructing them at the venue eliminates any risk of them popping in transit.

column9: At 35, hailing from West Philly,  joe Jiggs learned that laughter isn’t just entertainment its survival. After battling a range of health issues joe turned to comedy into his medicine. He uses the stage to process pain, find perspective, and connect with others who know life doesn’t always go as planned.  Joe style of comedy is real, unfiltered, and rooted in everyday struggles.  When your body puts you through it sometimes the only thing you can control is how hard you laugh back. Joe brings that energy to every set. Does his best to turn setbacks into punchlines and proving that even on your worst days, there’s still something funny hiding in the chaos.
column10: Sharp humor, real life perspective, and unfiltered storytelling . After going through a kidney and pancreas transplant, Joe turned his second chance at life into material that’s raw, real, and unexpectedly hilarious. From dating struggles to gym life to navigating everyday chaos, nothing’s off limits. His comedy feels like a conversation you’re not supposed to laugh at but you do anyway. It feels like a therapy session just funnier.

column8: Prudence Blowhole, Amy K Bormet, Matt Dievendorf
column9:
Prudence Blowhole is a cabaret artist, cryptozoology expert, romance novel enthusiast, unlicensed sex therapist, kooky aunt, sing-a-long initiator, and guest lecturer at a bar near you. With assistance from her band of jazz professors (Amy K Bormet and Matt Dievendorf), they create genre-fluid, surprisingly well-researched,  musical dissertations on a variety of saucy subjects. Their first show, Sex Monster, has attracted the attention of perverts across the Washington, D.C. area, selling out shows at historic jazz clubs, queer bars, and haunted mansions. Sex Monster was awarded a project grant in 2025 and 2026 by the D.C. Commission on the Arts and Humanities, which receives funding from the National Endowment for the Arts. Instagram: @prudenceblowhole

What real humans have said about Sex Monster:
“One of the funniest things I have ever seen.”
“Rabidly feminine.”
“Like a one-woman show, but not annoying.”
column10: “Sex Monster” is a musical dissertation by cryptozoology expert and perverted chanteuse Prudence Blowhole. Through a series of original, genre-spanning songs, Prudence and her band, The Music Department, demonstrate how monsters can not only improve your sex life, but lead to self-actualization. Lovingly referred to as “Lilith Fair for perverts,” this rabidly-feminine cabaret may encourage spectators to read fairy smut, howl at the moon, and proposition ghosts.
column11: We have honed our act in our hometown, and now we are eager to take our act on the road (Amtrak NE Regional). We love Philly and would love to build an audience there.  Festivals are a great way for us to meet new people!
column12:
I really appreciate that there is no submission fee! Totally understand why some producers need to implement that, but was happy to see this one was free to submit!

We are band! Vocals, Keys, and Guitar! We are at our coolest as a full group, but if you cannot accommodate live instruments, we have a tracked version of the show that's just me (Prudence) with a vocal mic, and that's still pretty cool.
